sources_audited.note: The cited Census NAICS page was reachable, but its current rendered content exposed only the generic NAICS search interface and 2022 manual link, not the asserted 541990 activity list. This weakens source verification for lens membership but does not independently establish a different eligible-share estimate.
The BLS table is explicitly for NAICS 541900 and lists photographic services and veterinary services among the industries within that broader aggregate, supporting the stated limitation.

BLS supports the cited inspection, testing-device, logging, reporting, physical, and judgment tasks and says remote inspections reduce inspector need while safety and quality sustain demand. It describes construction and building inspectors, not the narrowed 541990 population.

DOE documents Savannah River Site drone inspections across tanks, piping, power distribution, and other assets, with reduced labor, access risk, time, and cost and continued personnel review. It is one federal-site example and does not quantify typical commercial workflow exposure or five-year adoption.

The PHMSA project supports development and field validation of multisensor UAS collection, image processing, condition classification, and deep-learning video review for routine pipeline inspection. It is a completed research project, not evidence of representative lower-middle-market deployment or realized labor savings.
PHMSA directly states that the rule requires post-extreme-weather pipeline inspections and strengthens integrity-management assessment requirements.

PHMSA reports a large transmission and gathering mileage base through 2025 and a roughly stable transmission total. Mileage is an installed-base measure and does not establish outsourced inspection volume; gathering-series comparability also changes sharply beginning in 2022.

EY supports global TIC growth of 4-5% annually, major-player cost pass-through, AI as a strategic imperative, and nearly 1,000 global acquisitions over twelve years. It does not directly measure US lower-middle-market physical-inspection implementation, AI-benefit retention, completed qualifying transfers, or real demand growth.

Gallup directly reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years in its fall 2024 survey. The measure is cross-industry intent and includes transfers rather than completed qualifying control sales.

caveats: The cited Census URL did not expose the claimed 541990 activity detail in its current rendered page, so the factual membership basis for the narrowed lens was not independently verifiable from S1 during this audit; e is already labeled ESTIMATE, and no alternative value supported a tier or action change.

The a, rho, and o bases translate one federal-site drone example, one pipeline R&D project, and a different inspector occupation into the narrowed commercial population. Those bridges and likely overstatement direction are disclosed, but the sources do not quantify representative workflow exposure, adoption, or operator-required share.
S8 is broad global TIC evidence dominated by larger players and supports direction rather than the quantitative rho, s5, q, or d5 values for US lower-middle-market physical-inspection firms.
The q base is an honest estimate: major-company pass-through of inflationary cost increases is not direct evidence that independent inspection firms retain 53% of AI-derived benefits over five years.

Pipeline mileage and integrity rules support recurring need but not outsourced purchased quantity across the full lens; the PHMSA gathering series also has a visible reporting break beginning in 2022.
Gallup measures intended sales or transfers across all employer businesses, not completed external control acquisitions of qualifying inspection firms; the haircut and this limitation are disclosed.
summary: Mechanics pass. The narrowed recurring physical-inspection lens is coherent and explicitly disclosed, and the narrative follows the cited directional evidence. The record remains highly assumption-dependent: most score-driving sources establish technical feasibility or broad sector direction rather than representative US lower-middle-market values, and the Census activity-detail citation was not verifiable in the rendered page. These weaknesses are honestly classified and caveated, and correcting or downgrading them does not supply a demonstrated mechanism that changes the base PRIORITY tier or the existing VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action, so there is no material finding under the brief's test.
